title: Python
description: Learn Python from your first print statement through variables, collections, functions, loops, and file handling.

lessons.title: Python Introduction
lessons.subtitle: What Python is, how to run code, and your first program
lessons.theory:
Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language created to be readable and productive. Its clear syntax makes it popular for beginners, data science, web backends, automation, and scripting.

You run scripts with python filename.py or python3 on some systems. The interactive REPL (read–eval–print loop) lets you type expressions and see results immediately—great for experiments.

Indentation defines blocks instead of braces. Use four spaces per level consistently. A colon starts blocks after if, for, while, def, and class headers.

Python 3 is the current standard. The community shares packages on PyPI and installs them with pip. The built-in help() and official docs support self-directed learning.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Python is an interpreted, dynamically typed language that emphasizes readable code and rapid development across many domains.

keyPoints: Readable syntax with significant indentation
Run scripts with python file.py or use the REPL
Python 3 is the modern standard
pip installs third-party packages from PyPI
The colon plus indent starts code blocks

codeExample.label: Hello, Python
codeExample.code:
# hello.py
print('Hello, LearnTheory!')
print(2 + 3)

# In the REPL: help(print)

quiz.question: How are code blocks defined in Python?
quiz.explanation: Python uses consistent indentation (usually four spaces) after a colon.

quiz.question: Which command typically runs a Python script file?
quiz.explanation: The interpreter is invoked as python (or python3) followed by the filename.

quiz.question: What does the REPL allow you to do?
options: Type code interactively and see immediate results
quiz.explanation: The REPL evaluates each expression as you enter it—ideal for quick tests.

lessons.title: Variables
lessons.subtitle: Assignment, naming rules, and updating values
lessons.theory:
Variables are names that refer to values. You create them with assignment: count = 0, message = 'LearnTheory'. No separate declaration keyword is required.

Names use snake_case by convention: user_name, total_score. They must start with a letter or underscore, followed by letters, digits, or underscores. Avoid reserved keywords like if, for, and class.

Assignment updates the name to point at a new value. With immutable types like int and str, rebinding is the norm. Mutable objects can change in place while the name stays the same.

Multiple assignment works: x, y = 1, 2. Swapping is elegant: a, b = b, a. Choose descriptive names so readers understand intent without comments.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A variable is an identifier bound to an object in memory; assignment creates or rebinding that name.

keyPoints: Assignment with = creates or updates a binding
snake_case is the standard naming style
Names are case-sensitive: Count and count differ
Avoid shadowing built-ins like list and str
Tuple unpacking assigns multiple names at once

codeExample.label: Variables and swapping
codeExample.code:
course = 'Python'
lessons_completed = 3
lessons_completed += 1

first, second = 'HTML', 'CSS'
first, second = second, first

print(course, lessons_completed, first)

quiz.question: Which variable name follows Python style guidelines?
quiz.explanation: PEP 8 recommends snake_case: lesson_count.

quiz.question: What does lessons_completed += 1 do?
options: Adds 1 and rebinds the name to the new value
quiz.explanation: += is augmented assignment: read value, add 1, assign back.

quiz.question: How do you swap two variables in one line?
quiz.explanation: Tuple unpacking swaps efficiently: a, b = b, a.

lessons.title: Data Types
lessons.subtitle: int, float, str, bool, and type checking
lessons.theory:
Python is dynamically typed: a name can refer to different types over time, and types are associated with objects, not variable declarations. Common built-ins include int, float, str, and bool.

int holds integers of arbitrary precision. float holds decimal numbers; beware rounding in financial math (use decimal module when precision matters). str is text; triple quotes span multiple lines.

bool values are True and False (capitalized). Comparisons and logic return booleans. None represents absence of a value, similar to null in other languages.

type(x) and isinstance(x, str) inspect types. Prefer isinstance for checks because it respects inheritance. Convert between types with int(), float(), str(), and bool().

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Data types classify values (objects) in Python, determining which operations are valid on them.

keyPoints: int, float, str, bool are core built-in types
True and False are capitalized boolean literals
None means no value or missing result
isinstance is preferred over type() for checks
f-strings format text: f'Score: {score}'

codeExample.label: Types and conversion
codeExample.code:
age = 21
price = 19.99
name = 'Ada'
active = True

print(type(age), isinstance(name, str))
score_text = str(95)
print(f'{name} is {age}, score {score_text}')

quiz.question: Which function checks if x is a string, including subclasses?
quiz.explanation: isinstance handles inheritance correctly; type() is stricter equality.

quiz.question: What is the boolean result of 5 > 3?
quiz.explanation: Comparison operators return True or False.

quiz.question: Which creates a formatted string with variables embedded?
quiz.explanation: f-strings evaluate expressions inside braces at runtime.

lessons.title: Lists
lessons.subtitle: Ordered mutable sequences, slicing, and comprehensions
lessons.theory:
Lists store ordered, mutable sequences: primes = [2, 3, 5, 7]. Index from 0; negative indices count from the end: items[-1] is the last element. Slicing [start:stop:step] copies subsequences.

append adds one item; extend adds many. insert places at an index; pop removes and returns. sort and reverse change order in place; sorted() returns a new sorted list.

List comprehensions build lists concisely: squares = [n * n for n in range(5)]. Add an if clause to filter: [n for n in range(10) if n % 2 == 0].

Lists can nest and mix types, but homogeneous lists are clearer. Copy with list(old) or old[:]—assignment alone shares the same list object.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A list is a mutable, ordered collection of items enclosed in square brackets, allowing duplicates and mixed types.

keyPoints: Indexes start at 0; -1 is the last item
append, extend, pop are common mutating methods
Slicing [1:4] returns a new sublist
Comprehensions combine loops and optional filters
Use copy techniques to avoid accidental aliasing

codeExample.label: Lists and comprehensions
codeExample.code:
topics = ['html', 'css', 'python']
topics.append('javascript')

first_two = topics[:2]
evens = [n for n in range(10) if n % 2 == 0]

print(topics, first_two, evens)

quiz.question: How do you add one item to the end of a list?
quiz.explanation: append adds a single element to the end of the list.

quiz.question: What does items[-1] return?
quiz.explanation: Negative indexing -1 refers to the last element.

quiz.question: What does [n * 2 for n in range(3)] produce?
quiz.explanation: range(3) is 0,1,2; doubled values are 0, 2, 4.

lessons.title: Dictionaries
lessons.subtitle: Key–value maps, access patterns, and iteration
lessons.theory:
Dictionaries map unique keys to values: user = {'name': 'Ada', 'score': 98}. Keys must be hashable—strings, numbers, tuples of immutables. Values can be any type.

Access with brackets user['name'] raises KeyError if missing. get('name', default) returns a default safely. Assign user['role'] = 'admin' to add or update.

keys(), values(), and items() views support iteration. for key, value in user.items() is idiomatic. dict comprehensions build maps: {n: n * n for n in range(3)}.

JSON objects deserialize to dicts in Python. Nested dicts model structured records. Since Python 3.7+, dicts preserve insertion order as an implementation standard.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A dictionary is a mutable mapping from hashable keys to arbitrary values, written with curly braces and key: value pairs.

keyPoints: Keys must be unique and hashable
get avoids KeyError with a default
items() loops key and value pairs together
Dict comprehensions create mappings concisely
JSON loads into Python dict structures

codeExample.label: Dictionary usage

quiz.question: How do you safely read a key that might be missing?
quiz.explanation: get returns the default instead of raising KeyError.

quiz.question: What does for k, v in d.items() iterate over?
quiz.explanation: items() yields tuples of (key, value) for each entry.

quiz.question: Which keys are valid in a dictionary?
options: Hashable immutables like strings and numbers
quiz.explanation: Keys must be hashable; lists cannot be keys, strings can.

lessons.title: Functions
lessons.subtitle: def, return, parameters, defaults, and docstrings
lessons.theory:
Functions group reusable logic. Define with def name(parameters): followed by an indented body. return sends a value back; omitting return gives None.

Parameters can have defaults: def greet(name='guest'). Keyword arguments at call time improve clarity: greet(name='Ada'). *args collects extra positional values; **kwargs collects extra keyword arguments.

Scope: variables assigned inside a function are local unless declared global (rare). Reading outer variables works in nested functions; assigning requires nonlocal in closures.

Docstrings are triple-quoted strings right after def. They document behavior and appear in help(). Type hints (name: str) -> int are optional documentation and tooling aids, not enforced at runtime by default.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A function is a named block of code defined with def that accepts parameters and optionally returns a value.

keyPoints: def introduces a function; colon and indent follow
return exits with a value; None is implicit otherwise
Default parameters apply when arguments are omitted
Docstrings document purpose and usage
Keyword arguments clarify calls with many parameters

codeExample.label: Function with defaults
codeExample.code:
def progress_label(completed, total=10):
    """Return a simple progress string."""
    pct = round(100 * completed / total)
    return f'{completed}/{total} ({pct}%)'

print(progress_label(3))
print(progress_label(7, total=8))

quiz.question: What does a function return if there is no return statement?
quiz.explanation: Python functions return None when they finish without return.

quiz.question: Where should a docstring be placed?
options: Before import statements in the file
Immediately after the def line as the first statement
quiz.explanation: The first statement in the function body should be the docstring.

quiz.question: What do default parameter values enable?
options: Calling the function without passing every argument
quiz.explanation: Defaults apply when the caller omits that parameter.

lessons.title: Loops
lessons.subtitle: for, while, range, break, continue, and iteration
lessons.theory:
for loops iterate over sequences and other iterables: for lesson in lessons: print(lesson). They work with lists, strings, dict keys, files, and generators.

range(n) produces 0 through n-1; range(start, stop, step) controls bounds and stride. while repeats while a condition is true—ensure the condition eventually becomes false.

break exits the loop immediately; continue skips to the next iteration. else on a loop runs if the loop completes without break—uncommon but useful in search patterns.

enumerate pairs indexes with items: for i, topic in enumerate(topics). zip combines parallel sequences. Avoid infinite while True without a clear exit path.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Loops repeat a block of code either for each item in an iterable (for) or while a condition holds (while).

keyPoints: for iterates any iterable, not only lists
range generates numeric sequences lazily
while needs a condition that eventually changes
break and continue alter loop flow
enumerate adds an index while looping

codeExample.label: for and while

quiz.question: What values does list(range(3)) produce?
quiz.explanation: range(3) yields 0, 1, 2 — up to but not including 3.

quiz.question: What does continue do inside a loop?
quiz.explanation: continue jumps to the next cycle without finishing the current body.

quiz.question: When is a while loop appropriate?
options: When you know the exact count always
When repetition depends on a condition that changes
quiz.explanation: while fits when you repeat until a condition becomes false.

lessons.title: File Handling
lessons.subtitle: Opening files, reading, writing, and context managers
lessons.theory:
Programs persist data by reading and writing files. open(path, mode) returns a file object. Common modes: 'r' read text, 'w' write (truncates), 'a' append, and 'rb'/'wb' for binary.

Always close files to flush buffers—better, use with open(...) as f: so Python closes automatically even if an error occurs. This is the context manager pattern.

read() loads the whole file; readlines() returns a list of lines. For large files, iterate line by line: for line in f. write() and writelines() output text.

Paths can be relative or absolute. pathlib.Path offers a modern API: Path('data.txt').read_text(encoding='utf-8'). Specify encoding='utf-8' for text files to handle international characters correctly on all platforms.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: File handling is reading from and writing to the filesystem using open or pathlib, usually with context managers for safe cleanup.

keyPoints: Use with open(...) as f for automatic closing
Specify encoding=utf-8 for text files
Modes control read, write, and append behavior
Iterate lines for memory-efficient reading
pathlib.Path simplifies paths and common operations

codeExample.label: Read and write a file
codeExample.code:
from pathlib import Path

log = Path('progress.txt')
log.write_text('Lesson 1 complete\n', encoding='utf-8')

with open('progress.txt', 'a', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    f.write('Lesson 2 complete\n')

print(log.read_text(encoding='utf-8'))

quiz.question: Why use with open('file.txt') as f?
options: It runs code faster than any other approach
It ensures the file is closed when the block exits
It converts files to JSON automatically
quiz.explanation: Context managers call close reliably, even after exceptions.

quiz.question: Which mode opens a file for writing and clears existing content?
quiz.explanation: 'w' truncates the file to zero length or creates it if missing.

quiz.question: What does encoding='utf-8' help with?
options: Correctly reading and writing Unicode text
quiz.explanation: UTF-8 encodes international characters in text files consistently.
